narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 43% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try—a risk level that exceeded our admission threshold. This prediction was based on key details from your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, LSAT score of 30, first-year decile rank of 4, third-year decile rank of 3, upper-middle family income bracket, and your status as a white female planning full-time enrollment. Compared to typical students, your LSAT is notably lower than the average of 36.7, and both your decile ranks fall below the dataset averages of around 5.5–5.7, while your GPA is very close to the typical 3.21 and your demographics align with common patterns like being white (83.8% of students) or female (43.6%). The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your third-year decile rank, which stood out as the top factor pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure. Your LSAT score was the next strongest influence in the same direction, followed closely by your first-year decile rank. Your family income bracket, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status each played smaller but still contributing roles, all nudging the outcome toward failure risk rather than success. In essence, no single feature pulled strongly in your favor; instead, these elements together painted a picture of elevated bar exam challenges relative to peers who typically pass. This ranking shows how academic performance markers like your deciles and LSAT carried the most weight over background factors. To improve your chances moving forward, focus on what you can realistically change: retake the LSAT to aim above 36, which could dramatically shift predictions since it's a top influencer. Strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework or graduate prep might also help, as year-three performance was pivotal here. Your GPA is already solid, so building bar-specific study habits early could address the overall risk profile. With targeted improvements like these, a future application could look much stronger to the model and admissions team. Keep pushing—you have a clear path ahead.
